text: A madrasa in Garissa, Kenya, where the suspected mastermind of the deadly attack on a nearby university teaches, has been closed due to the national mourning declared by President Uhuru Kenyatta after the attack. The Islamic religious school, called the Jamii Bora Academy, is located in a small town in northeastern Kenya. The man suspected of being the mastermind behind the attack, Mohamed Mohamud, once taught there, along with others including the principal, Sheikh Khalif Abdi Hussein. According to Sheikh Khalif, Mohamud was a quiet student who did not talk much, but was normal like everyone else until he joined Al-Shabaab. Mohamud is believed to be in command of a militia based near Kenya's border with Somalia, who are responsible for numerous cross-border attacks into Kenya. The Islamist militant group, allied with al Qaeda, has been waging a bloody campaign for control of Somalia. Kenya has now become a target as its troops are part of an African Union force deployed in support of Somalia's UN-supported government. At least one of the four gunmen who carried out the attack on the university is also believed to be Kenyan. The Kenyan government is concerned that Al-Shabaab is recruiting disaffected youth from inside the country.

text: The trial of Pedro Hernandez, the man charged with the 1979 murder of 6-year old Etan Patz, continues. Hernandez confessed to police in 2012 that he lured Patz into a basement and killed him, but his lawyers maintain that due to his mental health issues, including schizophrenia and an IQ in the range of borderline to mild mental retardation, Hernandez is unable to discern whether he committed the crime or not. His confession and waiving of his Miranda rights were ruled legal by a judge last November. A previous suspect, Jose Antonio Ramos, was deemed responsible for Patz’s death and ordered to pay the family $2 million in 2001, but he has never been charged with the crime. The Patz case led to a heightened awareness of missing children in the US, with Patz’s image appearing on milk cartons across the country and prompting Congress to pass the Missing Children's Assistance Act in 1984.

text: Etan Patz, a 6-year-old boy who went missing in New York City in 1979, is back in the news as a New York jury deliberates over the possible guilt of Pedro Hernandez, the man charged in his killing. Hernandez confessed to the crime three years ago, but his lawyer argues that he is mentally challenged and unable to discern whether he committed the crime or not. Patz's parents have waited a long time for justice, but doubts remain about Hernandez's culpability. Despite his confession, no physical evidence or remains have been found. Hernandez has been diagnosed with schizophrenia and has a low IQ. Another man, Jose Antonio Ramos, who was acquainted with Patz's babysitter, has also been linked to the case but has never been charged. The Patz family sued Ramos and won a $2 million settlement, but they have never received the money. The disappearance of Etan Patz sparked a nationwide awareness of crimes against children and led to the creation of the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children.

text: Etan Patz, a six-year-old boy who disappeared in New York City in 1979, has been at the center of one of the most widely publicized missing person cases in American history. For more than 35 years, the case of Etan Patz has remained unsolved, with no trace of the child or his killer ever found. On Friday, a New York jury will begin deliberating on the possibility of convicting Pedro Hernandez, a man who has confessed to the murder, for the killing of Etan Patz. However, some have questioned whether Hernandez is capable of understanding the gravity of the situation due to his severe mental illness. Hernandez's lawyer, Harvey Fishbein, has argued that his client suffers from schizophrenia and has an IQ in the borderline-to-mild mental retardation range. Despite the controversy surrounding Hernandez's case, the Patz family has worked tirelessly to keep the memory of their son alive and raise awareness about missing children in America. In the early 1980s, Etan's picture was featured on milk cartons across the country, sparking a national movement to find missing children. Although the number of children abducted and murdered has remained relatively low, the public's perception of the threat has increased significantly. This heightened awareness has led to changes in laws and law enforcement practices aimed at protecting children. The case of Etan Patz continues to inspire hope and determination in the fight against child abduction and murder in America.
